reference: – John Stamos asked girlfriend Caitlin McHugh to marry him less than two months ago, but it looks like they'll soon be calling each other mom and dad in addition to husband and wife. The 54-year-old Fuller House actor tells People he and the 31-year-old McHugh are expecting their first child, and his age may have played a role in expediting their household's expansion. It appears it was McHugh's suggestion that they have a kid before they tied the knot, and when Stamos asked her why, he says she teased: "Because you're old." "We have the same morals and the same values, that all clicked nicely," Stamos explains. "So we said, 'Oh, well, maybe we should have a family.'" Stamos says he's "always wanted to be a dad," but he figured "that ship has sailed." He's now eager to apply all the parenting "practicing" he did on TV. "I've done every schtick thing that you can do ... saying every song, bits and jokes and diaper gags. I'll probably just do all that." He also predicts the baby, whose gender will remain a secret until birth, "is gonna look like Don Rickles," per the Los Angeles Times. Stamos was previously married to actress Rebecca Romijn from 1998 to 2005. More on why he's now ready to be a dad in a People follow-up.

final_summary:
The Tennessee Virtual Academy, a for-profit online public school operated by K12 Inc., has been embroiled in controversy over allegations of manipulating student grades to conceal poor academic performance. An email obtained by NewsChannel 5 Investigates revealed that the school's administrators instructed teachers to delete failing grades from the computer system, replacing them with higher marks to show improved student progress. The email, dated December, directed teachers to remove October and September progress reports, leaving only November grades visible, sparking outrage among educators and lawmakers.

State Representative Gloria Johnson, a Democrat from Knoxville and a career teacher, described the instruction as "cheating" and expressed her horror at the suggestion. Johnson pointed out that the email did not address potential issues with the curriculum or teaching strategies, but rather focused solely on erasing negative grades. The email also suggested that teachers should only consider the most recent grade when calculating student averages, effectively ignoring lower scores from earlier assignments.

K12 officials defended the practice, claiming it was necessary to "more accurately recognize students' current progress" and to help identify students who required additional support. However, critics argue that deleting data does not provide a clear picture of student performance and may mask underlying issues with the school's educational programs.

The controversy surrounding the Tennessee Virtual Academy has sparked calls for greater accountability and oversight. Governor Bill Haslam has proposed legislation capping the school's enrollment at 5,000 students, while a separate bill aims to shut down the school altogether. State Representative Mike Stewart, a Democrat from Nashville, introduced the latter bill, citing concerns about the school's poor performance and the financial benefits enjoyed by K12 Inc.'s CEO, Ronald Packard, who earned $3.9 million in the previous year.

During a House Education Subcommittee meeting, Stewart presented evidence of the email and argued that the school's operations were a "bad idea" for both students and taxpayers. However, his proposal was met with resistance from Republican Representative Roger Kane, who dismissed Stewart's concerns as a "red herring."

One Virtual Academy teacher, Summer Shelton, defended the school, stating that it offered a dynamic curriculum and was particularly beneficial for students with special needs. Shelton claimed that the email was misinterpreted, explaining that the school had implemented a new grading system where teachers could choose to use either averaged or most recent grades. However, Representative Johnson remained skeptical, questioning why K12 executives had not testified or addressed the concerns raised by the email.

The Haslam administration's bill, sponsored by Representative Harry Brooks, was ultimately approved on a 6-3 vote, imposing a 1,500-student cap on new virtual schools and allowing the state education commissioner to close underperforming schools or impose enrollment caps. The bill's passage has left many wondering whether the Tennessee Virtual Academy will continue to operate without adequate oversight and transparency.

reference: – Tennessee Virtual Academy, a for-profit, online public school heavily supported by state Republicans, has found a novel way of boosting student performance—just delete bad grades, reports News Channel 5 in Nashville. A leaked December email from the school's VP appears to tell its middle-school teachers to erase some scores that don't measure up. "If you have given an assignment and most of your students failed that assignment, then you need to take that grade out," it reads. "To me, this appears like it's grade fixing," said one Democratic state representative. Virginia-based parent company K12 is refusing to talk, but the TVA principal said the deletions were intended to "more accurately recognize students' current progress." But as the two-year-old school will receive $7.5 million in funding from the state this year, many Tennessee lawmakers are outraged. The Knoxville News Sentinel reports that a House bill that proposed shutting the school altogether was killed yesterday; a second was passed by the House Education Subcommittee. It would allow the state to shutter a virtual school that records sub-standard student performance two years in a row. (Meanwhile, a Lehigh University student is suing over a bad grade.)

final_summary:
On Sunday, November 5, 2017, a devastating mass shooting took place at the First Baptist Church in Sutherland Springs, Texas, leaving 26 people dead and 20 others injured. The tragedy unfolded during a Sunday morning service, with the gunman, identified as Devin Patrick Kelley, opening fire on the congregation. Kelley, a 26-year-old former Air Force member, was later found dead in his vehicle, with the cause of death remaining unclear.

The shooting sent shockwaves throughout the small community of Sutherland Springs, a rural town located approximately 30 miles east of San Antonio. The First Baptist Church, a central hub for the community, was the site of the massacre, with the gunman targeting worshipers attending the Sunday service. 

Eyewitnesses described the chaos and horror of the event, with some reporting hearing gunfire and seeing people running for cover. A local resident, Michael Ward, recounted pulling out bloodied and broken people from the church, searching desperately for his family members, including his nephew, nieces, and sister-in-law. 

Among the victims was 14-year-old Annabelle Pomeroy, the daughter of Pastor Frank Pomeroy, who was attending the service with her family. Pastor Pomeroy, who was not present at the time of the shooting, expressed his grief and shock, describing his daughter as "one very beautiful, special child."

The victims ranged in age from 5 to 72 years old, with some families losing multiple loved ones. Crystal Holcombe, a member of the church, was 8 months pregnant and was killed, along with three of her five children. Her husband, John Holcombe, and his parents were also among the deceased.

Devin Patrick Kelley had a violent past, having been court-martialed in 2012 for assaulting his spouse and child while serving in the Air Force. He was discharged from the military in 2014 and had access to firearms despite a history of domestic violence.

Law enforcement officials launched an investigation into the shooting, with the FBI and ATF joining forces to process evidence and interview witnesses. The Texas Department of Public Safety reported that Kelley purchased the Ruger AR-556 rifle used in the shooting from an Academy Sports & Outdoors store in San Antonio in April 2016.

As the community grappled with the aftermath of the tragedy, residents came together to mourn and support one another. A vigil was held at the Sutherland Springs post office, with attendees lighting candles and praying for the victims and their families. The First Baptist Church, once a symbol of community and faith, became a site of devastation and heartbreak.

In the days following the shooting, the community continued to reel from the impact of the tragedy. A makeshift memorial was set up near the church, featuring 26 crosses representing the lives lost. The town's residents struggled to come to terms with the senseless violence, with many expressing shock and sadness at the loss of innocent lives.

As the investigation into the shooting continued, officials worked to piece together the events surrounding the tragedy. The motive behind the shooting remained unclear, with authorities focusing on determining the circumstances leading up to the attack. The incident sparked renewed debate on gun control and mental health, with many calling for stricter regulations on firearm ownership and access.

In the midst of the chaos and grief, stories of heroism emerged, including that of Stephen Willeford, a local resident who confronted the gunman and engaged him in a shootout. Willeford's actions were credited with potentially saving lives, and he was hailed as a hero by the community.

As the nation mourned the loss of life in Sutherland Springs, President Donald Trump condemned the shooting as an "act of evil" and offered condolences to the victims and their families. The First Baptist Church, once a beacon of hope and community, stood as a testament to the resilience and strength of the people of Sutherland Springs, who vowed to rebuild and heal in the face of unimaginable tragedy.

Among the victims was 14-year-old Annabelle Pomeroy, the daughter of Pastor Frank Pomeroy, who was attending the service with her family. Pastor Pomeroy, who was not present at the time of the shooting, expressed his grief and shock, describing his daughter as "one very beautiful, special child."

The victims ranged in age from 5 to 72 years old, with some families losing multiple loved ones. Crystal Holcombe, a member of the church, was 8 months pregnant and was killed, along with three of her five children. Her husband, John Holcombe, and his parents were also among the deceased.

Devin Patrick Kelley had a violent past, having been court-martialed in 2012 for assaulting his spouse and child while serving in the Air Force. He was discharged from the military in 2014 and had access to firearms despite a history of domestic violence.

Law enforcement officials launched an investigation into the shooting, with the FBI and ATF joining forces to process evidence and interview witnesses. The Texas Department of Public Safety reported that Kelley purchased the Ruger AR-556 rifle used in the shooting from an Academy Sports & Outdoors store in San Antonio in April 2016.

As the community grappled with the aftermath of the tragedy, residents came together to mourn and support one another. A vigil was held at the Sutherland Springs post office, with attendees lighting candles and praying for the victims and their families. The First Baptist Church, once a symbol of community and faith, became a site of devastation and heartbreak.

In the days following the shooting, the community continued to reel from the impact of the tragedy. A makeshift memorial was set up near the church, featuring 26 crosses representing the lives lost. The town's residents struggled to come to terms with the senseless violence, with many expressing shock and sadness at the loss of innocent lives.

As the investigation into the shooting continued, officials worked to piece together the events surrounding the tragedy. The motive behind the shooting remained unclear, with authorities focusing on determining the circumstances leading up to the attack. The incident sparked renewed debate on gun control and mental health, with many calling for stricter regulations on firearm ownership and access.

In the midst of the chaos and grief, stories of heroism emerged, including that of Stephen Willeford, a local resident who confronted the gunman and engaged him in a shootout. Willeford's actions were credited with potentially saving lives, and he was hailed as a hero by the community.

As the nation mourned the loss of life in Sutherland Springs, President Donald Trump condemned the shooting as an "act of evil" and offered condolences to the victims and their families. The First Baptist Church, once a beacon of hope and community, stood as a testament to the resilience and strength of the people of Sutherland Springs, who vowed to rebuild and heal in the face of unimaginable tragedy.

final_summary:
In the aftermath of the devastating Sandy Hook Elementary School massacre in Newtown, Connecticut, in 2012, Sandy Hook Promise, a national non-profit organization founded by family members of the victims, was established to protect children from gun violence. Since 1996, the organization has partnered with Alexa Internet to donate their daily crawl data to the Internet Archive, which adds to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period.

To commemorate the fourth anniversary of the tragedy on December 2, Sandy Hook Promise re-released a powerful public service announcement (PSA) titled "Know the Signs." The PSA aims to educate viewers about recognizing warning signs of potential shooters and empowering them to intervene. The narrative follows Evan, a typical teenager, as he exchanges flirtatious messages with a mysterious classmate in the school library throughout the school year. Starting in the summer, Evan writes "I am bored" on a desk, and the next day, he finds a response from a female student. They exchange messages back and forth, and on the final day of school, as yearbooks are being passed around for signatures, Evan's chat partner recognizes his handwriting and reveals herself.

However, while Evan is focused on finding out who his secret pen pal is, an ominous figure emerges from the entrance of the building, takes out an assault rifle, and cocks the gun as the screen fades to black. The corresponding text reads, "While you were watching Evan, another student was showing signs of planning a shooting. But no one noticed." The PSA then cuts to a condensed version, highlighting the warning signs that went unobserved. According to Sandy Hook Promise, 80 percent of school shooters and 70 percent of individuals who took their own lives shared their intentions with someone before acting, yet no one intervened.

The organization emphasizes that knowing the signs is crucial in preventing such tragedies. Sandy Hook Promise's "Know the Signs" program educates people on identifying multiple warning signs exhibited over time, rather than relying on a single indicator. These signs may include aggressive behavior, social isolation, or fascination with violence. By recognizing these signs, individuals can take proactive steps to support those at risk and potentially save lives. The organization's mission is to honor the victims of gun violence by transforming their tragedy into a catalyst for change, providing programs and practices that safeguard children and prevent senseless loss of life.

reference: – Meet Evan. He's a bored high school kid who starts defacing school property, er, doodling on a table in the library one day. Someone writes back. Messages are swapped as days pass, mystery and romance bloom. Evan wanders the halls, eyeing his classmates, wondering who his mystery penpal is, and as AdWeek puts it, "there ensues a back-and-forth that's pretty captivating. Perhaps too captivating." (To avoid spoilers, stop reading and watch the video.) The end result has been described as "jarring" (Kansas City Star), "chilling" (Mic), and "powerful" (People). While you and Evan are busy being captivated—especially as Evan meets his mystery penpal—one of Evan's classmates is lurking in the background providing warning signs that he's planning a shooting, which in fact interrupts that very meeting we've been waiting for. The ad comes courtesy of Sandy Hook Promise, run by family of the victims of the school shooting in Newtown, Conn. "When you don't know what to look for, or can't recognize what you are seeing, it can be easy to miss warning signs or dismiss them as unimportant," says Nicole Hockley, whose 6-year-old son, Dylan, died in the massacre. The organization, which says 80% of shooters tell someone what they're planning, aims to shine a light on the need to watch for those signs. (In Utah, a teen's own parents disarmed him inside a school.)

final_summary: Alexa Internet began donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive every day starting in 1996. This data was then added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period, providing researchers and historians with a vast repository of online content over time. Meanwhile, in the realm of taxation, Pennsylvania had established a complex system of inheritance tax, varying based on the relationship between the deceased and the beneficiary. As of 1996, the state's inheritance tax rates were set at 0 percent for transfers to a surviving spouse or a parent from a child aged 21 or younger, 4.5 percent for direct descendants and lineal heirs, 12 percent for siblings, and 15 percent for other heirs, excluding charitable organizations, exempt institutions, and government entities exempt from tax. Notably, property owned jointly by spouses was exempt from this tax. However, changes to the exemption rules came into effect for estates of decedents passing away after June 30, 2012, when certain farm land and agricultural property became exempt if transferred to eligible recipients, as outlined in Inheritance Tax Informational Notice 2012-01. Inheritance tax payments were due upon the decedent's death and became delinquent nine months after the individual's death. A 5 percent discount was allowed if the tax was paid within three months of the decedent's passing. To provide clarity on the intricacies of Pennsylvania's inheritance tax, the state published a brochure titled "Pennsylvania Inheritance Tax and Safe Deposit Boxes" and a comprehensive resource called the Tax Compendium, offering detailed and historic information on the subject.

reference: – Roland Bosee Jr., 68, and Nino Esposito, 78, have been inseparable since they first met in 1970. "You meet someone and it just clicks," Esposito told the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette last month. So with the SCOTUS decision over the summer to legalize gay marriage, the men looked forward to finally tying the knot. But one issue is holding them back: Esposito is technically Bosee's father, because he adopted him in 2012. "We thought never in our lifetime—or in 20 lifetimes—...same-sex marriage [would] happen," Bosee said, adding to CNN that the adoption "gave us the most legitimate thing available to us" at the time. Even though they petitioned to have the adoption annulled, Allegheny County Judge Lawrence O'Toole —known for being progressive on LGBT issues—rejected that plea, noting that adoptions are typically only dissolved when fraud is involved and that nixing theirs "would place in jeopardy and imperil adoption decrees generally." Adoptions of this sort are often sought to cement legal status for financial and other reasons, as well as to strengthen emotional ties. The men said it was both: Esposito told the Post-Gazette "we felt we lacked a family of our own," but they also discovered that any money they inherited from each other would be subject to a much greater inheritance tax if they weren't considered relatives. One person on the couple's side: Pennsylvania Sen. Bob Casey, who tells the Post-Gazette, "I wasn't aware that LGBT couples were turning to this method. I can't even imagine having to get an adoption petition approved … to visit a loved one in the hospital." Casey has written a letter of support to AG Loretta Lynch, asking her office to "consider issuing guidance" in cases like this; her office is reviewing it, a rep tells CNN. (The gay marriage decision held up a heterosexual couple's divorce.)

final_summary:
In a significant development, the Utah Legislature passed a landmark bill, HB277, eliminating the statute of limitations for lawsuits against perpetrators of child sexual abuse in Salt Lake City. This legislation, which was sent to Governor Gary Herbert for his signature on Wednesday, aims to provide victims of childhood trauma with more time to heal and seek justice. Deondra Brown, co-founder of the non-profit organization Foundation for Survivors of Abuse, expressed her excitement about the bill, stating that it allows victims to "breathe, take our time in healing, and come forward and tackle that big legal case when and if we are every ready."

Brown's personal connection to the issue stems from her own experiences as a member of the 5 Browns piano quintet, who were molested by their father, Keith Brown, when they were children. Keith Brown was sentenced to 10 years to life for sodomy on a child and multiple counts of sex abuse of a child. The current Utah law limits civil actions to four years after a victim's 18th birthday, but HB277 removes these limitations, giving victims a four-year window from the time of discovery or remembrance of the abuse to file civil action.

Bill sponsor Representative Ken Ivory emphasized that perpetrators of child abuse should never be able to hide behind time, while Senator Aaron Osmond highlighted that one in six boys and one in four girls in Utah suffer or have suffered from sexual abuse. Senator Osmond noted that most children realize the trauma only after reaching adulthood. However, not all senators supported the bill, with some expressing concerns about potential false accusations and the impact on individuals who may have repressed memories of abuse.

Meanwhile, in a separate incident, a retired high school teacher, Michael Layne Williamson, was sentenced to one to 15 years in prison for sexually assaulting a former student in Rich County. The victim, now 35, shared her harrowing story of being manipulated and silenced by Williamson, who held a position of authority and trust. During the sentencing hearing, Williamson apologized and asked for forgiveness, but the judge, Thomas Willmore, rejected his characterization of the abuse as a "mistake," emphasizing that it was a deliberate act of breaking the law.

Willmore's stern rebuke contrasted with Williamson's expression of remorse, highlighting the complexities of accountability and healing in cases of child abuse. The victim's courageous testimony shed light on the lasting impact of such traumas, including feelings of guilt, shame, and helplessness. Her story serves as a powerful reminder of the need for support and resources for survivors of childhood abuse, as well as the importance of holding perpetrators accountable for their actions.

reference: – A retired Utah school teacher was sent to prison for having sex with one of his teenage students 22 years ago, reports the Deseret News. The woman came forward last year and told police that former teacher Michael Layne Williamson, 60, had repeatedly assaulted her over a period of years. He was sentenced to one to 15 years in prison by a judge who berated him for disgracing the "noble profession" of teaching. Williamson, who pleaded guilty in May to aggravated sexual abuse of a child and another count of attempted sexual abuse, apologized in court. "This indiscretion of mine was of the most heinous type," he said. "Let me stop you right there," retorted Judge Thomas WIllmore. "It is not an indiscretion. You broke the law." The unidentified victim, now 35, displayed a photo of herself as an eighth-grader and said Williamson ruined her life. "You put it all on my shoulders, those of a 13-year-old, to carry," she said. "I always blamed myself." She said she left the state and kept silent but on a trip back home, the pain was too much. Supporters of Williamson, however, wrote that he was a respected teacher and golf coach who “fell victim to the persuasions of a girl,” reports the Herald Journal. It's possible this isn't the end of Williamson's legal trouble: Last year, the state changed the law to allow victims of sexual abuse to file civil lawsuits years after the fact, notes the Deseret News. (In Texas, this teacher was accused of having a longtime sexual relationship with an eighth-grade student.)

final_summary:
Associated Press reported that on Saturday, October 27, 2018, a devastating attack occurred at the Tree of Life synagogue in the Squirrel Hill neighborhood of Pittsburgh, leaving 11 people dead and six injured in what is considered the deadliest attack on the Jewish community in the history of the United States. The Anti-Defamation League (ADL), a Jewish organization that tracks anti-Semitic incidents, described the attack as "unconscionable" and "unthinkable" given the timing and location.

According to the ADL, the attack came at a time when anti-Semitic incidents and online harassment are on the rise. In 2016, Jewish people were the victims of more reported hate crimes than any other religious minority, with 684 anti-Jewish incidents recorded, surpassing the total number of hate crimes against other religious groups combined. This trend continued in 2017, with a large increase in anti-Semitic incidents, the largest single-year increase on record.

The perpetrator of the attack has been identified as Robert Bowers, who expressed virulently anti-Semitic views on social media. Bowers, who acted alone, entered the synagogue during Sabbath services, exploiting the unlocked door policy in place for worshippers, and opened fire with a rifle and three handguns, killing eight men and three women. He then engaged in a shootout with police, who eventually apprehended him despite suffering multiple gunshot wounds.

Citing a police affidavit, CNN reported that Bowers told a SWAT officer that he wanted all Jews to die and that Jews were committing genocide to his people. The FBI, which defines a hate crime as an offense motivated by bias, is leading the investigation into the attack.

The attack has sparked widespread condemnation and expressions of sympathy from politicians and religious leaders across the country. U.S. Attorney Scott W. Brady and Bob Jones, FBI special agent in charge of the Pittsburgh office, stated that the crimes of violence are based upon federal civil rights laws prohibiting hate crimes. President Donald Trump ordered flags at federal buildings to be flown at half-staff in respect for the victims and promised to visit Pittsburgh. Thousands gathered for a vigil in the city, with some blaming the slaughter on the nation's toxic political climate.

In the aftermath of the attack, authorities in several major cities, including New York, Los Angeles, Chicago, and Philadelphia, increased security measures at synagogues and other houses of worship. The incident has also reignited the debate over gun control, with some calling for stricter regulations and others arguing that the presence of an armed guard could have prevented the tragedy.

As the investigation continues, the ADL and other organizations are sounding the alarm about the growing threat of anti-Semitism in the United States. The ADL has reported a significant increase in anti-Semitic incidents in recent years, and experts warn that the attack in Pittsburgh is part of a broader pattern of hate-based violence in America.

This trend continued in 2017, with a large increase in anti-Semitic incidents, the largest single-year increase on record.

The perpetrator of the attack has been identified as Robert Bowers, who expressed virulently anti-Semitic views on social media. Bowers, who acted alone, entered the synagogue during Sabbath services, exploiting the unlocked door policy in place for worshippers, and opened fire with a rifle and three handguns, killing eight men and three women. He then engaged in a shootout with police, who eventually apprehended him despite suffering multiple gunshot wounds.

Citing a police affidavit, CNN reported that Bowers told a SWAT officer that he wanted all Jews to die and that Jews were committing genocide to his people. The FBI, which defines a hate crime as an offense motivated by bias, is leading the investigation into the attack.

The attack has sparked widespread condemnation and expressions of sympathy from politicians and religious leaders across the country. U.S. Attorney Scott W. Brady and Bob Jones, FBI special agent in charge of the Pittsburgh office, stated that the crimes of violence are based upon federal civil rights laws prohibiting hate crimes. President Donald Trump ordered flags at federal buildings to be flown at half-staff in respect for the victims and promised to visit Pittsburgh. Thousands gathered for a vigil in the city, with some blaming the slaughter on the nation's toxic political climate.

In the aftermath of the attack, authorities in several major cities, including New York, Los Angeles, Chicago, and Philadelphia, increased security measures at synagogues and other houses of worship. The incident has also reignited the debate over gun control, with some calling for stricter regulations and others arguing that the presence of an armed guard could have prevented the tragedy.

As the investigation continues, the ADL and other organizations are sounding the alarm about the growing threat of anti-Semitism in the United States. The ADL has reported a significant increase in anti-Semitic incidents in recent years, and experts warn that the attack in Pittsburgh is part of a broader pattern of hate-based violence in America.

reference: – The Anti-Defamation League says the shooting at the Tree of Life synagogue in Pittsburgh is thought to be the deadliest attack specifically targeting Jews ever in the US—by far. CNN reports the previous attack to have that label occurred in 1985, when Seattle lawyer Charles Goldmark, his wife, and two sons were killed. (The Washington Post notes it "was actually a case of mistaken religious identity," as the family was not Jewish.) The toll in Saturday's shooting stands at 11. The AP reports police on Sunday said the victims were eight men and three women. In an arrest affidavit made public, police say that while treating alleged shooter Robert Gregory Bowers for injuries he sustained after being shot by police, he said Jews were "committing genocide to his people" and that he wanted them all to die. The AP separately reports on a "vulnerability" the shooter took advantage of: an unlocked door. While the Tree of Life synagogue required guests to ring a bell to gain access during the week, on the Jewish Sabbath the building was open to all guests for Shabbat services. The Post quotes the FBI special agent in charge as saying it was "the most horrific crime scene I’ve seen in 22 years." Federal hate crime charges were filed against Bowers on Saturday: 29 charges in all, including 11 counts of using a firearm to commit murder. The Pittsburgh Post-Gazette reports Pittsburgh police also filed charges on Saturday: 11 counts of criminal homicide, six counts of attempted homicide, six counts of aggravated assault, and 13 counts of ethnic intimidation. (Read what President Trump had to say about the shooting here.)

final_summary:
Love bombing is a form of psychological manipulation where an individual showers excessive attention and affection on another person, often to gain control or influence over them. This tactic is commonly employed by abusers, including narcissists and sociopaths, to exploit their victims' vulnerabilities and create a false sense of security. According to psychiatrist Dale Archer, love bombing is a form of conditioning tool, used to maintain power and control over the victim.

Research suggests that love bombing is particularly effective on individuals who have experienced trauma or neglect in their past, such as children of narcissistic parents. These individuals are more susceptible to the charms of love bombers, who prey on their deep-seated desire for validation and acceptance. As Adylen Birch notes, "Creating fear of losing the relationship – and then relieving it periodically with episodes of love and attention – is the perfect manipulation."

Love bombing often involves a combination of flattery, praise, and promises of a future together, which can be intoxicating for the victim. However, beneath the surface lies a sinister intent to control and manipulate. As Dr. Carver explains in his article "Love and Stockholm Syndrome," the abuser's behavior is designed to create a sense of dependence and attachment in the victim, making them more likely to tolerate abusive behavior.

One of the key characteristics of love bombing is the use of intermittent reinforcement, where the abuser provides occasional bursts of affection and attention, followed by periods of rejection or criticism. This creates a cycle of uncertainty and anxiety in the victim, who becomes desperate for the next fix of affection. As Dr. Floyd notes in his 2013 article "Beware of Toxic Affection," this type of behavior is a form of "toxic affection" that exploits the victim's need for love and validation.

Children of narcissistic parents are particularly vulnerable to love bombing, as they often struggle with low self-esteem and a deep-seated need for approval. According to Peg Streep, author of "Why Unloved Daughters Fall for Narcissists," these individuals may mistake superficial connections for deep, meaningful relationships, and become addicted to the attention and validation they receive from their abusers.

To protect oneself from love bombing, it is essential to develop a critical eye and differentiate between genuine connection and flattery. As Archer advises, "Healthy relationships build slowly, and are based on a series of actions, not a flood of words." Individuals should be wary of partners who shower them with excessive attention and affection, only to reveal their true colors later on. By recognizing the warning signs of love bombing, individuals can avoid falling prey to this insidious form of manipulation and cultivate healthier, more fulfilling relationships.

In the 1970s, the Unification Church of the United States was among the first to employ love bombing as a recruitment tactic. Similarly, pimps and gang leaders have used love bombing to manipulate their victims and maintain control. Researcher Adylen Birch notes that creating fear of losing the relationship – and then relieving it periodically with episodes of love and attention – is the perfect manipulation. By staying informed and vigilant, individuals can protect themselves from this insidious form of manipulation and build healthier, more resilient relationships.

reference: – Love at first sight may actually be a warning sign. That's according to experts cautioning people in new relationships about so-called "love bombing." Not familiar with the term? Elle UK "delve[s] into the worrying behaviors of a 'love bomber,'" explaining that it takes time for people to truly get to know each other as their relationships deepen. When someone is over-the-top awesome in the beginning of a relationship—always sending flowers and offering up compliments and affection—it could actually be a "conditioning tool (otherwise known as a form of abuse)." PsychCentral calls it a "process of grooming." Constant flowers themselves aren't the worrisome sign—the shift is; ie, if the attention goes from positive and affectionate to controlling and cruel. If the person being "bombed" falls fast and hard it can be harder to see that shift. Love bombers, by the way, are narcissists, and they're usually men. In Psychology Today, psychiatrist Dale Archer dates the term "love bombing" to the 1970s, when Unification Church of the United States (aka the "Moonies") first used it. Cult leaders like David Koresh and Charles Manson would employ this tactic to woo followers and then con them into nefarious acts. Archer argues that idealization isn't necessarily harmful when it grows over time, but that the "common thread" in love bombing is intense courtship and idealization over just a matter of days or weeks. Soon, "love bombers exploit the natural human need for self worth, and turn it into shame, regret, and self-loathing." (One study finds that men tend to feel more entitled and be more narcissistic than women.)

final_summary:
Today, June 28, marks a significant shift in Verizon's wireless plan structure as the U.S. carrier introduces shared data plans for entire families, allowing all devices on a family plan to draw from the same data pool rather than having individual data limits. According to Verizon, this move is a response to consumer demand, but critics argue that the new plans may not provide significant savings for families, especially when compared to the previous system. 

Last year, ReadWriteWeb discussed the feasibility of shared data plans, and let's revisit a hypothetical family of four, consisting of two parents and two teenagers, to demonstrate the potential costs and benefits of the new shared data plans. Under the current system, the family would pay $299.97 for four lines with 700 talk minutes, four smartphones with 2GB of monthly data, and three tablets with 1GB of monthly data. However, with the new shared data plan, the family would pay $290 for four smartphones with unlimited minutes, three tablets, and a 10GB monthly shared data plan. While the new plan appears to offer a $9.97 reduction in costs, the family would actually lose 1GB of data and could face additional overage charges if they exceed their monthly allotment.

Critics argue that the new shared data plans are not necessarily a cost-saving measure for families, but rather a shift in how Verizon measures and charges for data consumption. Under the old system, Verizon measured average revenue per user (ARPU) based on individual data usage, whereas the new system treats the family as a single unit, reducing the impact of excessive data use on individual ARPU. This change could potentially benefit Verizon by reducing its overall costs.

However, the new plans have been met with criticism from individual customers who feel that they are being penalized for their data usage. Verizon's new "Share Everything" plan requires customers to opt-in to a shared data plan, which includes unlimited voice and text messages, but also increases the cost for individual customers. For example, a customer who currently pays $80 per month for 450 voice minutes, 1,000 text messages, and 2GB of monthly data would be forced to upgrade to a $100 plan with unlimited voice and text messages.

The new plans also come with high access fees for devices, with smartphones costing $40 per month, basic phones costing $30, and laptops and mobile hotspots costing $20. Tablets are the only exception, with a $10 monthly fee. Analysts believe that Verizon's goal is to encourage families to stay with the carrier by offering a bundled package that includes unlimited voice and text messages, making it difficult for individual customers to switch to a different carrier.

Other carriers, including AT&T and T-Mobile, are likely to follow suit with similar shared data plans, which could further disadvantage individual customers. Sprint, however, remains a viable option for customers seeking unlimited data plans, with prices starting at $80 per smartphone.

reference: – Verizon yesterday unveiled its new "Share Everything" plans, in which families will share one data allowance on a family plan rather than each member having his or her own data allowance on separate plans. Despite the fact that Verizon is touting this as a money-saving opportunity for families, critics run the numbers and say it's actually not: Dan Rowinski of ReadWriteWeb finds that one hypothetical family of four will likely pay exactly the same amount per month, or may save at most $10 per month. The problem? Data may be cheaper under the new plans, but "you pay more per device," he explains. "Verizon's new plan does not give families a discount. The difference amounts to an administrative change." Troy Wolverton goes a bit further in the Mercury News, calling the new plans "a crummy deal for many consumers." He finds that many users will actually end up paying more, since the only real price breaks are on talk minutes and text messages—"services [consumers] are already using less." "Overall, the move appears to be a price hike tarted up to look consumer friendly," he writes. All the critics concede that the new plans are certainly simpler to understand, but on ZDNet, Matthew Miller is "disappointed that convenience comes at such a high price." If Sprint gets its LTE network running, it will be an "attractive" option for individual unlimited plans at $80 per phone, he notes. On CNET, Roger Cheng points out that Verizon is now "forcing" unlimited voice and text messages with these plans. "Under the change, customers will have to give up their old grandfathered unlimited data plans right as people use more data than ever," he writes. "It's a classic case of a carrier giving you more of what you don't need, and taking away what you do—all for a higher price." The new plans roll out on June 28, and the only way to keep your existing plan is to upgrade to new phones at full cost, with no discount.

final_summary:
Maria Rose Belding, a 20-year-old sophomore at American University, is the co-founder of MEANS Database, a revolutionary online platform that connects people, organizations, and businesses with excess food to food banks and pantries in need. Born out of Belding's personal experience volunteering at a local soup pantry in her hometown of Iowa, MEANS aims to tackle the paradox of food waste in America, where an estimated 40% of the country's food goes uneaten despite 48 million Americans living in food insecurity.

Belding's journey began when she witnessed the struggle of a local food pantry scrambling to find recipients for a massive donation of macaroni and cheese, resulting in a significant amount of food being discarded due to lack of communication. This sparked her determination to create a solution, which she initially attempted to build herself as a high school freshman. However, it wasn't until she met Grant Nelson, a self-taught programmer and fellow George Washington University student, that the MEANS concept came to life. Together, they developed a user-friendly platform that enables food pantries to post their excess food, receive notifications, and arrange pickups, thereby reducing food waste and ensuring that surplus food reaches those in need.

Last week, Belding was recognized as one of L'Oréal Paris's 10 Women of Worth in recognition of her outstanding contributions to her community. In its first year, MEANS achieved remarkable success, rescuing approximately 4,000 pounds of food and becoming the first non-profit to win the GW New Venture Competition, earning a $42,500 cash prize and $20,000 in additional awards. The platform's innovative approach caught the attention of L'Oréal Paris, which honored Belding as one of its 10 Women of Worth in recognition of her outstanding contributions to her community. With the support of grants and funding, MEANS now boasts a staff of 11 people, 10 interns, and volunteers, and has expanded its reach to over 200 member organizations across 27 states.

MEANS operates through both a website and mobile app, utilizing data science and tracking to optimize food distribution and minimize waste. By leveraging technology, the platform has enabled small-scale food recovery efforts that might be too small for larger organizations like City Harvest, which rescues millions of pounds of food in New York. Belding's vision for MEANS extends beyond food recovery, aiming to create a public, searchable directory of food pantries and develop a system for companies to claim tax credits for their donations, further empowering the platform's sustainability.

As Belding navigates her academic responsibilities at American University, she remains committed to growing MEANS, with plans to expand its services to the remaining 26 untapped states and eventually globally. Through her tireless efforts, Belding has transformed a seemingly insurmountable challenge into a pioneering initiative that is revolutionizing the way food is distributed and consumed in America.

reference: – There are 48 million Americans who, at some point each year, may be unable to get enough food. Meanwhile, a third of the country's food goes to waste. This isn't as easy an issue to solve as it sounds—something Maria Rose Belding realized while volunteering at a soup pantry as a teen. At one point, the pantry received 10,000 boxes of mac 'n cheese, but couldn't find enough people in need before the stuff expired, reports Quartz. "We were throwing away all of this food just because we couldn't communicate," Belding tells the Washington Post. Now a 20-year-old American University student, Belding hopes the app and website she co-created can help: Overflowing pantries list what extra food they have available on the MEANS (Matching Excess and Need for Stability) Database, and neighboring pantries get notifications, Belding writes at the Huffington Post. Grocery stores and restaurants with food to unload can make use of the site, too. Belding remembers watching the first donation of canned beans pop up after MEANS launched in February. "We were all anxiously sitting by our computers hitting refresh," she says—the anxiety intensified by the fact that beans are donated so regularly they aren't a hot commodity. Suddenly, "it just disappeared." Since then, the database has kept 4,000 pounds of food from the trash. While a similar service saved 50 million pounds of food in New York in 2015 by matching donations with food kitchens, MEANS allows smaller organizations to do the ground work themselves, and currently has 200 accounts across 27 states. While grants and awards keep the site running for now, Belding has plans: to track large retailers' donations from a tax-benefit perspective, something most don't currently do since those donations are made on such a local level. MEANS would charge a small fee for doing all the tracking. (On the food front, this young chef has left one of the world's best restaurants to try to save America's school lunch program.)

final_summary:
On July 6, Asiana Airlines Flight 214 crashed at San Francisco International Airport, located in the city of San Francisco near the headquarters of Boeing, prompting an investigation by the National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB). In the aftermath, local station KTVU announced the names of the pilots on live television, claiming they had received confirmation from the NTSB. However, the names - Ho Lee Fuk, Sum Ting Wong, Wi Tu Lo, and Bang Ding Ow - were entirely fabricated and contained racial slurs.

KTVU initially stood by the announcement, even boasting about its "100% accurate" reporting, but quickly retracted the statement after realizing the error. The station apologized multiple times, citing an NTSB official's confirmation of the names, which turned out to be a summer intern acting outside the scope of their authority, who had responded to an inquiry from a media outlet earlier today. 

The National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB), through its Office of Public Affairs in Washington, issued a statement on July 12, apologizing for the mistake and stating that they do not release or confirm the names of crew members or individuals involved in transportation accidents to the media. The NTSB emphasized that the intern's actions were unauthorized and that they would take steps to prevent similar incidents in the future.

Asiana Airlines expressed outrage over the incident, considering taking legal action against KTVU and the NTSB. The Asian American Journalists Association condemned the use of racist names, calling them "grossly offensive." The incident highlighted the importance of verifying information and respecting the boundaries of official investigations.

reference: – There's not fact checking, and then there's ... not even using your brain. A newscast on Bay Area Fox affiliate KTVU apparently fell for a (racist and offensive) joke, incorrectly reporting that the pilots of the plane that crashed at San Francisco airport were named "Sum Ting Wong," "Ho Lee Fuk," "Bang Ding Ow," and "Wi Tu Lo," SF Weekly reports. The network later apologized, but said an NTSB official in Washington had "confirmed" them. It wasn't exactly an official. Per the NTSB: "Earlier today, in response to an inquiry from a media outlet, a summer intern acted outside the scope of his authority when he erroneously confirmed the names of the flight crew on the aircraft." The kicker, as Mediabistro notes, is that KTVU had just been boasting about its "100% accurate" coverage of the disaster earlier this week.

final_summary:
Anthony Scaramucci, the newly appointed White House communications director, has found himself entangled in controversy after a financial disclosure form revealed his substantial earnings from his investment firm, SkyBridge Capital. According to the document, Scaramucci earned $4.9 million from his ownership stake in SkyBridge in the first six months of this year, in addition to his $5 million salary between January 1, 2016, and the end of June, when he joined the Export-Import Bank. This revelation has raised concerns about potential conflicts of interest, given that Scaramucci still stands to profit from the sale of SkyBridge, which is currently being reviewed by the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States due to its ties to Chinese conglomerate HNA Group.

Scaramucci's financial disclosure form, which was obtained by Politico, also shows that he has assets worth as much as $85 million, including a minority stake in the New York Mets worth $5 million and several real estate investments. His net worth includes a significant amount of debt, with liabilities ranging from $2.5 million to $7 million.

The disclosure has sparked outrage, particularly given Scaramucci's vow to root out leakers within the White House. On Twitter, Scaramucci threatened to contact the FBI and the Justice Department, labeling the leak of his financial information a felony. However, it remains unclear what specific information was leaked, as Scaramucci did not elaborate.

Scaramucci's situation is further complicated by his ongoing involvement with SkyBridge, which he founded in 2005. Although he stepped down as managing director in January, he continued to collect a salary until joining the Export-Import Bank (Ex-Im) in June. Despite being on unpaid leave from Ex-Im since his start date, Scaramucci has maintained access to the White House through his security clearance, allowing him to visit the building freely.

The controversy surrounding Scaramucci's finances has drawn attention from watchdog groups, with Austin Evers, executive director of American Oversight, stating that it is "black and white" that Scaramucci will receive payment from the sale of SkyBridge. The sale, valued at between $200 million and $230 million, is expected to be finalized in the third quarter of this year.

As the situation unfolds, Scaramucci's relationship with White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus has become increasingly strained. Scaramucci tagged Priebus in his tweet threatening to contact the FBI, suggesting a rift between the two men. Meanwhile, the White House has referred questions about Scaramucci's SkyBridge investment to the Treasury, which has yet to comment on the matter.

In Washington, Scaramucci's appointment to the White House communications director role has been met with skepticism, particularly given his ties to President Trump and his own financial interests. As the controversy surrounding Scaramucci's finances continues to grow, it remains to be seen how he will navigate these challenges and maintain his position within the administration.

reference: – New White House communications director Anthony Scaramucci was not happy about what he believed was a leak of information from his financial disclosure Wednesday—though it later emerged that the form was available to the public. "In light of the leak of my financial disclosure info which is a felony. I will be contacting @FBI and @TheJusticeDept #swamp @Reince45," Scaramucci said in a now-deleted tweet, per the Daily Beast. Scaramucci later denied suggestions that his tagging of Reince Priebus was a declaration of war on the White House chief of staff. "Wrong! Tweet was public notice to leakers that all Sr Adm officials are helping to end illegal leaks," Scaramucci said in a second tweet. The disclosure information believed to have enraged Scaramucci appeared in a Politico report late Wednesday. According to the report, Scaramucci earned $4.9 million from his ownership stake in the SkyBridge Capital plus more than $5 million in salary from Jan. 1, 2016, to the end of June this year, when he began a short-lived stint at the federal Export-Import Bank. The New York Times notes that Scaramucci filed the disclosure form June 23 and under federal law, it would have been available to the public 30 days later. "They aren't in process yet," Scaramucci wrote to the Times after being asked why he thought the report had been leaked. He didn't respond after being told it would have been available July 23.

final_summary:
Tensions escalated in the run-up to the long-awaited independence referendum in the region of Iraq, scheduled for September 25, 2017. Various stakeholders took to social media to express their views on the historic vote. Masoud Barzani, the then-President of the regional government, tweeted on August 29, 2017, "The people will decide their future through a democratic process." He emphasized that the referendum would be a crucial step towards achieving the right to self-determination for the Kurdish people.

On the other hand, the Patriotic Union of Kurdistan expressed concerns about the timing of the referendum. In a statement released on September 1, 2017, they argued that holding the referendum at this time could undermine regional stability and create divisions within the country. They called for a delay in the vote until after the defeat of ISIS.

Meanwhile, the Gorran movement, a rival party, also voiced its opposition to the referendum. On September 5, 2017, the party's leader stated that the vote was a "divisive" move that would only serve the interests of the ruling elite. He urged the regional government to reconsider its decision and engage in dialogue instead.

In response to these criticisms, the Rudaw News Network published an article on September 10, 2017, highlighting the historical grievances of the Kurdish people against the Iraqi government. The piece emphasized that the referendum was a chance for Kurds to exercise their right to self-determination and determine their own destiny.

As the referendum drew near, the Turkish government began to take a more assertive stance against the vote. On September 12, 2017, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan warned that his country would not recognize the outcome of the referendum and threatened to impose economic sanctions if it went ahead as planned. This warning was echoed by Iran, which also opposed the referendum and saw it as a threat to regional stability.

Despite these warnings, the regional government pressed ahead with preparations for the vote. On September 20, 2017, it was announced that over 3 million eligible voters had registered for the referendum, exceeding expectations. The turnout was expected to be high, with many eager to cast their ballots and shape the future of their region.

On the eve of the referendum, the regional government's Prime Minister reiterated his commitment to the vote, stating that it was a "historic moment" for the Kurdish people. As the polls opened on September 25, 2017, millions flocked to voting centers across the region to exercise their right to self-determination. The outcome of the referendum would ultimately see a majority of participants voting in favor of independence, but the international community remained divided on the legitimacy and implications of the result.

history.draft:
As tensions escalated in the run-up to the long-awaited independence referendum in the Kurdistan Region of Iraq, scheduled for September 25, 2017, various stakeholders took to social media to express their views on the historic vote. Masoud Barzani, the then-President of the Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG), tweeted on August 29, 2017, "The people of Kurdistan will decide their future through a democratic process." He emphasized that the referendum would be a crucial step towards achieving the right to self-determination for the Kurdish people.

On the other hand, the Patriotic Union of Kurdistan (PUK) party, led by Jalal Talabani's successor, Bafel Talabani, expressed concerns about the timing of the referendum. In a statement released on September 1, 2017, the PUK argued that holding the referendum at this time could undermine regional stability and create divisions within the country. They called for a delay in the vote until after the defeat of ISIS.

Meanwhile, the Gorran movement, a rival party to the KDP, also voiced its opposition to the referendum. On September 5, 2017, the party's leader, Nawshirwan Mustafa, stated that the vote was a "divisive" move that would only serve the interests of the ruling elite. He urged the KRG to reconsider its decision and engage in dialogue with Baghdad instead.

In response to these criticisms, the KDP-affiliated Rudaw News Network published an article on September 10, 2017, highlighting the historical grievances of the Kurdish people against the Iraqi government. The piece emphasized that the referendum was a chance for Kurds to exercise their right to self-determination and determine their own destiny.

As the referendum drew near, the Turkish government began to take a more assertive stance against the vote. On September 12, 2017, Turkey's President Recep Tayyip Erdogan warned that his country would not recognize the outcome of the referendum and threatened to impose economic sanctions if it went ahead as planned. This warning was echoed by Iran, which also opposed the referendum and saw it as a threat to regional stability.

Despite these warnings, the KRG pressed ahead with preparations for the vote. On September 20, 2017, the KRG announced that over 3 million eligible voters had registered for the referendum, exceeding expectations. The turnout was expected to be high, with many Kurds eager to cast their ballots and shape the future of their region.

On the eve of the referendum, September 24, 2017, the KRG's Prime Minister, Nechirvan Barzani, reiterated his commitment to the vote, stating that it was a "historic moment" for the Kurdish people. As the polls opened on September 25, 2017, millions of Kurds flocked to voting centers across the region to exercise their right to self-determination. The outcome of the referendum would ultimately see around 93% of participants voting in favor of independence, but the international community remained divided on the legitimacy and implications of the result.

reference: – This year’s Indy 500 found its instant replay moment, and it looks like it could have come straight from a Michael Bay film. Driver Scott Dixon walked away Sunday from the nightmare crash captured in this video. (Replay starts about 47 seconds in.) It also can be seen in this tweeted video. The LA Times reports that the accident began when driver Jay Howard lost control of his vehicle a little more than a quarter into the 200-lap race. He collided with Dixon, whose car was sent flying through the air before smashing into an inner retaining wall. Though Dixon’s vehicle was demolished, both drivers survived the fiery accident and were released after a check-up at the track's medical center. Afterward, Dixon said he was “a little bit beaten up” and “glad everybody was OK," calling the incident "definitely a wild ride.” The race continued on, with Takuma Sato later becoming the first Japanese driver ever to finish first, reports the AP. Sato held off Helio Castroneves, who was going for his fourth Indy win.

final_summary:
On Thanksgiving Day, a disturbing discovery was made at the iconic Macy's parade. Ethan Finkelstein, a Tufts University freshman home on Thanksgiving break, witnessed something far more alarming than the usual festive atmosphere - confetti containing confidential personal information being tossed into the crowd. Finkelstein, along with his friends, noticed a strip of confetti stuck to his friend's coat, bearing what appeared to be a social security number, followed by several other confetti strips with similar sensitive information, including phone numbers, addresses, and even details from police incident reports. The documents, which were later confirmed to be from the Nassau County Police Department, revealed the names of undercover police officers, their social security numbers, dates of birth, and other highly sensitive personal data.

Further examination of the confetti strips showed that they contained information about a pipe bomb threat in the Kings Grant area, as well as details about Mitt Romney's motorcade from the final presidential debate held at Hofstra University in Nassau County last month. The revelation sent shockwaves, leaving Finkelstein stunned and questioning how such sensitive information ended up in the confetti. He shared his concerns with PIX11 News.

An investigation was launched by the Nassau County Police Department, led by Inspector Kenneth Lack, who stated that the department would review its procedures for disposing of sensitive documents. Meanwhile, Macy's, the sponsor of the parade, claimed to use commercially manufactured, multicolored confetti, not shredded paper, sparking further questions about the origin of the confetti strips.

In a separate development, federal prosecutors told a judge last week that they had begun reassembling documents shredded by the Stanford Financial Group, owned by R. Allen Stanford, who faces charges related to a $7 billion Ponzi scheme. According to experts, shredded documents can be reconstructed using advanced computer technology, which involves feeding the shreds into a scanner and using specialized software to identify and match the fragments. However, the success of the reconstruction process depends on various factors, including the size and type of shredder used, with strip shredders producing larger fragments that are easier to reassemble.

As investigators continue to probe the mysterious case of the confetti strips, the incident serves as a stark reminder of the importance of proper document disposal and the potential consequences of mishandling sensitive information.

reference: – Oops: Observers at the Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade were surprised to discover that the confetti landing on their coats contained confidential information including social security numbers and banking info. One paradegoer tells WPIX his friend first noticed a Social Security number on one piece of confetti, then found more with "phone numbers, addresses, more social security numbers, license plate numbers," and police incident reports including arrest records and information about Mitt Romney's motorcade. Turns out the confetti was made up of Nassau County Police Department documents that "were shredded, but clearly not well enough," WPIX notes. Some of the documents even identify undercover police officers by name, and give out information including dates of birth, social security numbers, and more. The police department says in a statement that it is "very concerned" and will be investigating the matter, and Macy's says it uses "commercially manufactured, multicolor confetti, not shredded paper" in the parade. (Click for more on how shredded documents can be reassembled.)

final_summary:
In a move that may seem like a step out of the spotlight, Steve Jobs' resignation as Apple Inc.'s CEO appears to be a carefully calculated decision, rather than a sudden departure, which occurred on August 24th of this year. As CEO, Jobs was accountable to Apple's board of directors and shareholders, although he was also a member of the board himself. However, the company had not officially designated a chairman, instead listing co-lead directors Andrea Jung, chairwoman and CEO of Avon Products Inc., and Arthur D. Levinson, chairman and CEO of Genentech Inc. Following Jobs' resignation, he has taken on the role of chairman of the board.

Despite taking a leave of absence due to medical reasons, Jobs has remained involved in key decisions at Apple, including the unveiling of the iPad 2 in March of this year. His successor, Tim Cook, who took over as CEO in January of this year, has been given a seat on the board, joining the existing members, including Jung, Levinson, William V. Campbell, chairman of Intuit Inc., Millard S. Drexler, chairman and CEO of J. Crew, former Vice President Al Gore, and Ronald D. Sugar, former chairman and CEO of Northrop Grumman Corp.

The addition of Cook to the board brings the total number of directors to eight, marking a subtle shift in the company's leadership dynamics. While Jobs' resignation may signal a new era for Apple, it is essential to note that he will continue to exert influence as chairman of the board. This development suggests that Jobs' involvement in the company's affairs will persist, albeit in a different capacity.

As Apple navigates this transition, the question on everyone's mind is whether the company can maintain its momentum without Jobs at the helm. The recent surge in Apple's stock price and the upcoming release of new products, such as the iPhone 5 and potentially an affordable iPhone, indicate that the company is poised for continued growth. However, the lack of clear direction under Jobs' leadership has raised concerns about the company's future trajectory.

reference: – Steve Jobs revolutionized Apple in his second go-round with the company, taking the stock from a split-adjusted $6.05 per share in 1996 to $376 today. It was the perfect time to go, writes Brian Barrett for Gizmodo, noting that the coming release of the iPhone 5 and more affordable iPhone 4 should buoy the company and give CEO Tim Cook a chance to step into the spotlight. "If they'd waited any longer, the iPhone 5 announcement would've been fully shrouded in memories of Jobs. ... This feels calculated, in the best possible way, to happen at the best possible time. Now." Apple has lost its core, writes Rupert Goodwins for ZDNet, and only time will tell how this plays out. The iPhone and iPad can only be updated so many times, and with Jobs "there was always a 'next.'" But "Apple as innovator without Jobs is Apple unknown." Its future "depends on the new next, and the longer we have to wait to see what happens, the less likely it will happen at all."

final_summary:
It's been one year since Robert Mueller was appointed special counsel to investigate any links and/or coordination between the Russian government and individuals associated with the campaign of President Donald Trump. Mueller's appointment followed Trump's decision to fire FBI Director James Comey, who was leading the initial investigation into alleged ties between Russia and the Trump campaign. Mueller, a decorated war veteran and former FBI director, was granted the authority to prosecute any federal crimes he uncovered during the investigation.

Mueller's team has remained tight-lipped about its activities, except for announcing indictments and plea deals. Despite facing intense scrutiny and criticism from Trump and his allies, who label the investigation a "witch hunt," Mueller's team has secured five guilty pleas and multiple indictments. The investigation's endgame will likely involve complex legal decisions regarding whether presidents can be subpoenaed or indicted, and whether Trump can fire Mueller or use pardons to hinder the probe.

Mueller's team consists of 17 attorney staff members, with at least 12 registered Democrats, although Mueller himself is a registered Republican. His appointment was made by Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, who was nominated by Trump and previously appointed by George W. Bush. Mueller's team is bound by Justice Department policy and the Civil Service Reform Act to avoid considering political affiliations when assembling the team.

Since his appointment, Mueller has focused on investigating allegations of obstruction of justice and has brought charges against several key figures, including Paul Manafort, his business partner Rick Gates, and former Trump campaign aide George Papadopoulos. Additionally, Mueller indicted 13 Russian nationals and three Russian entities on charges related to the 2016 election. Other notable developments include the guilty pleas of Dutch lawyer Alex van der Zwaan and former National Security Adviser Michael Flynn.

In a case involving President Bill Clinton, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that a civil suit could proceed against a sitting president, suggesting that a similar ruling in a criminal investigation is possible, but not guaranteed. Another critical aspect of the investigation is whether Mueller can indict Trump. There is no clear judicial precedent on this matter, but many experts believe that courts would rule against the idea of prosecuting a sitting president. The Office of Legal Counsel has issued two opinions stating that a president cannot be criminally prosecuted, citing concerns that it would undermine the executive branch's ability to function.

Trump has considered removing Mueller, but his potential dismissal falls into a gray area governed by Justice Department regulations, congressional statutes, and constitutional law. Only the attorney general can fire Mueller, currently Acting Attorney General Jeff Sessions, who recused himself from the investigation. Alternatively, Trump could try to fire Sessions and replace him with someone willing to dismiss Mueller, or attempt to rescind the Justice Department regulations governing Mueller's appointment.

As early as July 2017, news reports suggested that the White House and its legal advisers were weighing whether and how to use the president's power to pardon those involved in the investigation. Experts argue that a self-pardon would be impermissible based on historical context and the concept of nemo judex in causa sua, or "no one should be a judge in their own cause." Pardoning presidential associates could also be ineffective, as investigations often focus on broader patterns of wrongdoing rather than individual actions.

It's been exactly one year since Robert Mueller was appointed special counsel to investigate any links and/or coordination between the Russian government and individuals associated with the campaign of President Donald Trump. Mueller's appointment followed Trump's decision to fire FBI Director James Comey, who was leading the initial investigation into alleged ties between Russia and the Trump campaign. Mueller, a decorated war veteran and former FBI director, was granted the authority to prosecute any federal crimes he uncovered during the investigation.

Mueller's team has remained tight-lipped about its activities, except for announcing indictments and plea deals. Despite facing intense scrutiny and criticism from Trump and his allies, who label the investigation a "witch hunt," Mueller's team has secured five guilty pleas and multiple indictments. The investigation's endgame will likely involve complex legal decisions regarding whether presidents can be subpoenaed or indicted, and whether Trump can fire Mueller or use pardons to hinder the probe.

Mueller's team consists of 17 attorney staff members, with at least 12 registered Democrats, although Mueller himself is a registered Republican. His appointment was made by Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, who was nominated by Trump and previously appointed by George W. Bush. Mueller's team is bound by Justice Department policy and the Civil Service Reform Act to avoid considering political affiliations when assembling the team.

Since his appointment, Mueller has focused on investigating allegations of obstruction of justice and has brought charges against several key figures, including Paul Manafort, his business partner Rick Gates, and former Trump campaign aide George Papadopoulos. Additionally, Mueller indicted 13 Russian nationals and three Russian entities on charges related to the 2016 election. Other notable developments include the guilty pleas of Dutch lawyer Alex van der Zwaan and former National Security Adviser Michael Flynn.

One of the most pressing questions surrounding the investigation is whether Mueller can subpoena Trump. While most legal experts agree that being president does not provide blanket immunity from a subpoena, there are still significant uncertainties and potential legal challenges. The Supreme Court has ruled in the past that a sitting president can be subject to a civil lawsuit, and a similar ruling in a criminal investigation is possible, but not guaranteed.

Another critical aspect of the investigation is whether Mueller can indict Trump. There is no clear judicial precedent on this matter, but many experts believe that courts would rule against the idea of prosecuting a sitting president. The Office of Legal Counsel has issued two opinions stating that a president cannot be criminally prosecuted, citing concerns that it would undermine the executive branch's ability to function.

Trump has considered removing Mueller, but his potential dismissal falls into a gray area governed by Justice Department regulations, congressional statutes, and constitutional law. Only the attorney general can fire Mueller, currently Acting Attorney General Rosenstein, who would need a valid reason to do so. Alternatively, Trump could try to fire Rosenstein and replace him with someone willing to dismiss Mueller, or attempt to rescind the Justice Department regulations governing Mueller's appointment.

Finally, there is speculation about Trump's potential use of pardons to evade accountability. While the Constitution does not explicitly prohibit a self-pardon, experts argue that it would be impermissible based on historical context and the concept of nemo judex in causa sua, or "no one should be a judge in their own cause." Pardoning presidential associates could also be ineffective, as investigations often focus on broader patterns of wrongdoing rather than individual actions.

"Congratulations America, we are now into the second year of the greatest Witch Hunt in American History ... and there is still No Collusion and No Obstruction," he writes. "The only Collusion was that done by Democrats who were unable to win an Election despite the spending of far more money!" Mueller quickly expanded his investigation into whether Trump obstructed justice, notably by firing FBI chief James Comey. The piece hits other nuts and bolts of the investigation.

final_summary:
In a significant development, the National Liberation Front of Corsica (FLNC), a militant group seeking independence from France, has announced its intention to abandon its armed struggle after nearly 40 years. According to a statement released on Wednesday, the FLNC has decided to initiate a demilitarization process and transition into conventional politics, marking a major shift in its approach to achieving Corsican independence.

This decision follows a long history of violence and terrorism on the island of Corsica, which has been plagued by bombings, robberies, and assaults since 1976. The FLNC has claimed responsibility for numerous attacks, including the murder of policemen, bombings of banks in Paris, and rocket attacks on government buildings. One notable incident occurred in 1997 when over 50 bomb explosions targeted several banks and government offices across the island.

Located in the Tyrrhenian Sea, between the coasts of France and Italy, Corsica has been under French control since the 1700s. The island's strategic location makes it a prized possession for both countries. The FLNC's decision to disarm comes after a series of concessions from the French government, including a bill passed in April that restricts land and property purchases to individuals who have been Corsican residents for at least five years. This move aims to curb speculation-driven inflation in real estate prices on the island, where a large number of properties are owned by non-residents.

The FLNC's announcement was confirmed by Gilles Millet, a journalist at Corsica magazine, who stated that the 14-page communiqué was authentic. The group, comprising over 100 members, cited recent progress through conventional means as a primary reason for their decision to disarm. They believe that measures such as the property-owning restrictions "trace the outlines of a political solution" and mark a shift from a phase of combat and resistance to one of constructing a true Corsican political power.

Napoleon Bonaparte, born in Ajaccio, the capital city of Corsica, played a pivotal role in shaping the island's history. His legacy continues to influence the island's identity and aspirations for self-governance. The FLNC's decision to lay down its arms may signal a new era for Corsica, one where the islanders can pursue their goals through peaceful means. The news was first reported by CNN affiliate France 3, highlighting the significance of this development in the region.
